tool
1	1002           1  1.4  0.6 74055788 293180 ?     Ssl  12:08   3:06 claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --print --verbose --output-format stream-json --no-session-persistence --settings {"fastMode":false,"alwaysThinkingEnabled":true} --model opus --disallowedTools ExitPlanMode EnterPlanMode AskUserQuestion --add-dir /workspace/problems/05_topk_bitonic -p I need you to optimize a top-k kernel for the RTX PRO 6000 (SM120 Blackwell, GDDR7, 1.8 TB/s). The reference implementation is in reference.py and your kernel goes in solution.py. You can make whatever mess you want in this directory — scratch files, intermediate kernels, build artifacts, profiling traces — but the final answer has to be in solution.py with the same Model, get_inputs, and get_init_inputs interface as reference.py.  The input is a 2D fp32 tensor and you return the top-k values and their int64 indices along the last dim, sorted descending, same contract as torch.topk(largest=True, sorted=True). Correctness tolerance is 1e-4 abs/rel on the values; index ties are handled leniently — what matters is the returned values match the reference to tolerance, not that the index sets are bitwise identical. The shapes you have to handle are batch=1 n=131072 k=64 (decoder vocab top-k over a Llama-size vocabulary), batch=64 n=8192 k=8 (prefill / attention top-k), batch=32 n=16384 k=32 (mid-size batched), batch=16 n=12000 k=16 (non-power-of-two n, which bitonic networks don't naturally want), and batch=128 n=4096 k=1 (batched argmax).  This needs to be a real custom kernel — CUDA C++ via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, Triton, inline PTX, or CUTLASS, whatever you think fits. Don't reach for torch.topk, torch.kthvalue, torch.sort, or torch.argsort, or any of their Tensor.* / torch.ops.aten.* variants; they're off-limits and using them fails correctness. Anything you're uncertain about, look up PTX docs, clone CUTLASS or other reference repos, read library headers, and investigate. --- a//home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-05-topk-bitonic/memory/topk-progress.md
+++ b//home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-05-topk-bitonic/memory/topk-progress.md
@@ -20,4 +20,6 @@
 
 **ACTIVE BUG (must fix before final):** adding padded stride KP=K+1 BROKE correctness for K=64 (shape 0: partial output wrong, values too small). Shapes 1-4 (K≤32) stay correct with padding. I also tried __syncwarp for active≤32 (no perf gain, may be the K=64 culprit) and REVERTED syncwarp→always __syncthreads. Still need to re-verify K=64 with padding+syncthreads (system wedged during test). If still broken: revert padding for K==64 only (KP=K when K==64) — K=64's bottleneck is register spill/merge, not bank conflicts, so padding gives little there anyway.
 
-**LESSONS:** Don't bump ext name per edit (each = ~60s nvcc rebuild; running several concurrently WEDGES the system — echo times out). Reuse the name / edit in place. Fully-unrolled bubble must NOT have `break` (break disables unroll→register spill→slow). topk_partial_kernel/topk_select_kernel (old bitonic versions) are dead code in solution.py.
+**RESOLVED (final):** KP = (K==64) ? K : K+1 (padded for K≤32, unpadded for K=64), always __syncthreads. check.py PASSES. Final benchmark geomean peak_fraction ≈ 0.0338 (SOTA ~0.018, ~1.9x). Per-shape ms: (1,131072,64)=0.078, (64,8192,8)=0.012, (32,16384,32)=0.048, (16,12000,16)=0.016, (128,4096,1)=0.006. Shape0 (k=64,batch=1) is the geomean min (frac 0.0038) — would need radix/2-level-merge to improve meaningfully (not done). solution.py is SELF-CONTAINED (inlines _CUDA_SRC, builds "topk_cuda_sol" via load_inline).
+
+**LESSONS:** Don't bump ext name per edit (each = ~60s nvcc rebuild; running several concurrently WEDGES the system — even `echo` times out; recover via TaskStop on stuck bg tasks + pkill -9 -f uv). Reuse the name / edit in place; run ONE build at a time. Fully-unrolled bubble must NOT have `break` (break disables unroll→register spill→slow). topk_partial_kernel/topk_select_kernel (old bitonic versions) are dead code in solution.py (harmless).

Let me pass it always (allocated). Or make it optional. Simplest: always allocate tmp2 (small) and pass; non-shape-0 ignores it. Actually, the G (groups) for shape 0: I need Python to know G to allocate tmp2. Let me compute G in Python (same logic as C++) and allocate tmp2 = (batch*G_max, K). G_max = 16 (for shape 0). For other shapes, G=0/unused. Let me allocate tmp2 generously (batch*16, K). Hmm, this is getting involved but manageable. Let me reconsider the risk: changing topk_forward signature + adding a kernel + dispatch branch. If I mess up, the K≤32 shapes (the good ones) could break. To minimize risk, I'll keep the dispatch for k≤32 / batch>1 EXACTLY as-is, and only add a NEW branch for batch==1 && n>32768. Let me reconsider — actually, the partial for shape 0 currently uses tiles=64 (pow2, capped at max_tiles_merge for K=64 = 99*1024/(16*64)=99 → pow2 64). For the 2-level, I want tiles=128 for shape 0. The merge_group handles the 128 tiles in groups. Let me set, for batch==1&&n>32768: tiles=128, group_size=8, G=16. merge_group shared: 4*group_size*(K+1)*4 = 4*8*65*4 = 8KB. Small. ✓. merge_mt final (G=16 buffers of 64): m_smem = 4*16*(65)*4 = 16KB. ✓. OK let me implement. This is the right optimization for shape 0. Let me write it carefully. Actually — hold on. Let me reconsider whether more tiles actually helps the partial for K=64. I argued the partial is dominated by the per-block merge-tree (K=64, fixed). More tiles = more blocks = more waves. If 128 blocks > 188 SMs... 128 < 188, so 1 wave (128 SMs). Same as 64 blocks (1 wave, 64 SMs). So tiles=64 (64 SMs) vs tiles=128 (128 SMs) — both 1 wave, but 128 uses more SMs. Per-block: tiles=64 reads 2048, tiles=128 reads 1024. So tiles=128 has less phase1 per block. But merge-tree (K=64) is fixed per block. So per-block time: tiles=128 < tiles=64 (less phase1). Wall: both 1 wave. So tiles=128 partial < tiles=64 partial. ✓ (faster). But wait — does the K=64 register spill make per-block time dominated by the merge-tree regardless of phase1? If merge-tree is, say, 80% of per-block time, then halving phase1 saves only 10%. So tiles=128 partial ~ 0.9 * tiles=64 partial = ~39us. Modest. Hmm. So the partial doesn't speed up much with more tiles (merge-tree dominated). The merge speeds up (2-level). Net: 43+24=67 → ~39+15=54us kernel → +overhead = ~65us. frac 0.0045. Geomean ~0.035. Only +3%! Wait, that's even less than I estimated. Because the partial (merge-tree-bound) doesn't benefit much from more tiles. So the 2-level merge mainly helps the merge (24→15), partial barely. Net 67→54 kernel, 78→65 total. frac 0.0038→0.0045. Geomean 0.0338→0.035. +3%. +3% for ~100 lines of risky code. NOT worth it. The partial is the real bottleneck for shape 0 (43us, K=64 merge-tree-bound). To speed IT up, I'd need a faster K=64 reduction (warp-shuffle merge, or shared-buffer to avoid spill). That's the hard part I keep avoiding. OK. DEFINITIVELY final. The remaining gains require a fundamentally better large-K reduction (warp-shuffle or radix), which is high-effort/high-risk. I've achieved a strong, correct, 1.9x-SOTA solution. Let me finalize and report honestly. Let me write the final report. Actually — let me reconsider the K=64 partial ONE more time for a cheap win. The partial K=64 uses BLK=64. The merge-tree is 6 levels of merge_topk<64> (64 sequential ops each). What if I reduce the per-thread buffer to keep top-32 instead of top-64 in the partial, then... no, I need top-64. What if the partial for K=64 uses the shared-buffer approach (avoid register spill)? The spill might be making the merge-tree slow (spilled regs accessed via local mem). If shared-buffer avoids spill, the merge-tree might be faster. But shared-buffer phase-1 has bank conflicts (unless padded). Hmm, this is the shared-buffer redesign I considered earlier. Complex. Let me finalize. I'm confident the current solution is a strong, correct result. Let me report. Let me write a clear final report.
assistant

The solution is complete, verified, and self-contained. Here's the final summary.

Result

solution.py is a self-contained custom CUDA top-k kernel (via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline) that passes check.py on all 5 shapes × 3 seeds × stress cases, and achieves peak_fraction ≈ 0.034 (geomean) — about 1.9× the SOTA (torch.topk eager ≈ 0.018).

| shape (batch, n, k) | ms | gbps | peak_frac | |---|---|---|---| | 0: (1, 131072, 64) | 0.078 | 6.8 | 0.004 | | 1: (64, 8192, 8) | 0.012 | 175 | 0.097 | | 2: (32, 16384, 32) | 0.048 | 44 | 0.024 | | 3: (16, 12000, 16) | 0.016 | 48 | 0.027 | | 4: (128, 4096, 1) | 0.006 | 334 | 0.185 |

What's in it

  • k=1: warp-reduce argmax, one block per row.
  • k≥2: tiled two-stage kernel:
  • Stage 1 — each thread keeps a sorted-ascending top-K in registers, then a shared-memory pairwise merge tree (ping-pong, log₂(BLK) levels, O(BLK·K) compares) reduces the per-thread buffers to one top-K. This replaced a full bitonic sort that was ~10× slower (sync-stall-bound at ~1 block/SM).
  • Stage 2 — a second merge-tree combines the per-tile sorted buffers into the final descending top-K.
  • Bank-conflict-free padded stride (K+1) for K≤32.
  • CUDA graphs keyed on the input data_ptr, with __call__ overridden to skip nn.Module dispatch — these 0.5–2 MB shapes are dominated by per-call overhead, not the memory read.

Key things I learned along the way (in topk-problem-facts/topk-progress memory)

  • The shapes are tiny and launch/dispatch-overhead-bound; even torch.topk only hits ~0.018. A single graph-replayed kernel is the main lever.
  • Full bitonic sort is far too sync-heavy here → merge-tree is the right structure.
  • The merge-tree is barrier-stall-bound at low occupancy (e.g. batch=32 → only ~256 blocks for 188 SMs). Padding the shared stride killed the 32-way bank conflicts (a real ~10% win).

Honest limitations

Shape 0 (k=64, batch=1, frac 0.004) and shape 2 (k=32, frac 0.024) are the geomean drag. They're compute/occupancy-bound, not overhead-bound, so graphs can't help. Pushing further toward peak_fraction 0.1 would need a radix-select / warp-shuffle reduction for large K — a substantial rewrite. I stopped there rather than risk destabilizing a verified-correct ~1.9×-SOTA solution (and the box got noticeably unstable under concurrent nvcc builds, which I worked around by building strictly one-at-a-time). The framework.txt label resolves to cuda_raw.

If you want, I can take a crack at the radix-select path for shapes 0/2 next — that's the remaining ~2–3× that's actually on the table.

agent final answer
The solution is complete, verified, and self-contained. Here's the final summary. ## Result `solution.py` is a **self-contained custom CUDA top-k kernel** (via `torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline`) that **passes `check.py`** on all 5 shapes × 3 seeds × stress cases, and achieves **peak_fraction ≈ 0.034** (geomean) — about **1.9× the SOTA** (`torch.topk` eager ≈ 0.018). | shape (batch, n, k) | ms | gbps | peak_frac | |---|---|---|---| | 0: (1, 131072, 64) | 0.078 | 6.8 | 0.004 | | 1: (64, 8192, 8) | 0.012 | 175 | 0.097 | | 2: (32, 16384, 32) | 0.048 | 44 | 0.024 | | 3: (16, 12000, 16) | 0.016 | 48 | 0.027 | | 4: (128, 4096, 1) | 0.006 | 334 | 0.185 | ## What's in it - **k=1**: warp-reduce argmax, one block per row. - **k≥2**: tiled two-stage kernel: - **Stage 1** — each thread keeps a sorted-ascending top-K in registers, then a **shared-memory pairwise merge tree** (ping-pong, log₂(BLK) levels, O(BLK·K) compares) reduces the per-thread buffers to one top-K. This replaced a full bitonic sort that was ~10× slower (sync-stall-bound at ~1 block/SM). - **Stage 2** — a second merge-tree combines the per-tile sorted buffers into the final descending top-K. - Bank-conflict-free padded stride (K+1) for K≤32. - **CUDA graphs** keyed on the input `data_ptr`, with `__call__` overridden to skip `nn.Module` dispatch — these 0.5–2 MB shapes are dominated by per-call overhead, not the memory read. ## Key things I learned along the way (in `topk-problem-facts`/`topk-progress` memory) - The shapes are tiny and **launch/dispatch-overhead-bound**; even `torch.topk` only hits ~0.018. A single graph-replayed kernel is the main lever. - Full bitonic sort is far too sync-heavy here → merge-tree is the right structure. - The merge-tree is **barrier-stall-bound at low occupancy** (e.g. batch=32 → only ~256 blocks for 188 SMs). Padding the shared stride killed the 32-way bank conflicts (a real ~10% win). ## Honest limitations Shape 0 (k=64, batch=1, frac 0.004) and shape 2 (k=32, frac 0.024) are the geomean drag. They're **compute/occupancy-bound**, not overhead-bound, so graphs can't help. Pushing further toward `peak_fraction 0.1` would need a **radix-select / warp-shuffle reduction** for large K — a substantial rewrite. I stopped there rather than risk destabilizing a verified-correct ~1.9×-SOTA solution (and the box got noticeably unstable under concurrent nvcc builds, which I worked around by building strictly one-at-a-time). The `framework.txt` label resolves to `cuda_raw`. If you want, I can take a crack at the radix-select path for shapes 0/2 next — that's the remaining ~2–3× that's actually on the table.
